title: Metallic crossover through the tilt-free transition in La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$ at high pressure and temperature
authors: Bastien Michon, Yingpeng Yu, Beatrice D'Alò, Elena Stellino, Gergely Németh, Bosen Wang, Jianping Sun, Jinguang Cheng, Paolo Postorino, Ferenc Borondics, Francesco Capitani
abstract: La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_7$, a bilayer nickelate with Ruddlesden-Popper structure, undergoes a pressure-induced structural transition from a tilted Amam phase to an untilted Fmmm (or I4/mmm) phase near 10-15 GPa, concomitant with the emergence of high-T$_c$ superconductivity (T$_c$ $\sim$ 80 K). Despite intense interest, the phase boundaries and the impact of structural changes on the electronic properties remain unclear. Here, we combine high-pressure and high-temperature Raman and synchrotron-based infrared spectroscopies to map the structural and electronic evolutions. Raman measurements confirm the pressure-driven structural transition and reveal the emergence of Fano line shapes, indicating enhanced electron-phonon coupling. High-temperature data show analogous spectral signatures above 544 K, suggesting an unreported upper temperature limit of the Amam phase within the T-P phase diagram of this system. Infrared reflectivity measurements evidence a concomitant metallization, with a tremendous two-order-of-magnitude increase in carrier density, marking a crossover from a bad metal to a good metal. These results establish a unified picture of the structural transition and its strong coupling to the electronic properties.
summary: This paper demonstrates that a structural transition from a tilted to a tilt-free phase in La3Ni2O7 leads to a massive increase in charge carrier density. By using Raman and infrared spectroscopy, the authors establish a link between the lattice structure and a metallic crossover from a bad metal to a good metal. This provides a unified picture of the electronic and structural evolution in this high-Tc superconducting candidate.
main_problem: Determining the precise phase boundaries and the impact of structural changes on the electronic properties of the bilayer nickelate La3Ni2O7 under high pressure and temperature.
main_result: The study identifies a structural transition from a tilted Amam phase to a tilt-free phase, which drives a metallic crossover from a bad metal to a good metal with a two-order-of-magnitude increase in carrier density.
method: High-pressure and high-temperature Raman spectroscopy combined with synchrotron-based infrared and visible reflectivity measurements using a diamond anvil cell.
model_or_system: The physical system is La3Ni2O7, a bilayer nickelate with a Ruddlesden-Popper structure, studied under pressures up to 17 GPa and temperatures up to 544 K.
key_observables: Raman phonon modes, Fano line shape (electron-phonon coupling), plasma frequency (carrier density), and infrared reflectivity.
important_parameters: Pressure (0.4 to 17 GPa), Temperature (up to 544 K), and the structural phases (Amam, coexistence, and tilt-free).
main_assumptions: The reliability of the signal in the diamond phonon interference region is limited, requiring the exclusion of certain spectral ranges from fitting.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows phonon disappearance, softening, and increased Fano parameter; Figure 3 shows infrared reflectivity spectra and the shift of the plasma edge; Supplementary figures show the evolution of phonon FWHM, intensity, and the massive enhancement of carrier density (omega_p^2) with pressure.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the nickelate system and the problem of structural-electronic coupling, describes the experimental setup for high-pressure/temperature spectroscopy, presents Raman results tracking structural transitions and electron-phonon coupling, presents infrared results demonstrating the metallic crossover, and concludes with a unified phase diagram.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Structural transition observed in Raman spectra under high pressure and high temperature (laser heating). Temperatures, derived from anti-Stokes thermometry (Meth- ods), are rounded and correspond to the midpoints of their uncertainty ranges (see Fig. 2).

FIG. 3. Evidence for a metallic crossover from HP infrared reflectivity. The grayed region in panel (a) corresponds to strong absorptions by diamond phonons, which introduces significant noise and limits the reliability of the reference signal, particularly at HP (modifications in diamond phonon modes).

FIG. 4. T-P phase diagram constructed from pressure- and temperature-dependent Raman measurements. Black and green triangles indicate the onset temperatures of the DW- like order (TDW ) and superconductivity (Tc), respectively, with filled symbols for single crystals [6] and open symbols for polycrystals [7]. Pink and purple circles represent onset pressures and temperatures from our Raman data. The pink, purple and blue regions correspond to the Amam (bad metal), coexistence (Amam + tilt-free) and pure tilt-free (good metal) phases, respectively.
